techniques.name: Activate Firmware Update Mode
techniques.description: Adversaries may activate a firmware update mode on a device to enable loading of unauthorized firmware. Some devices have a mode for updating firmware that provides a window for loading modified or malicious firmware.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet used this technique on Siemens S7 PLCs.

techniques.name: Monitor Process State
techniques.description: Adversaries may gather information about the physical process state of an ICS environment to inform future attack stages. This may include reading process variable values from historians or OT protocols.
techniques.knownUse: Used in pre-Stuxnet reconnaissance and Industroyer operational preparation.

techniques.name: Automated Collection
techniques.description: Adversaries may use automated techniques to collect data from ICS systems, including configuration files, PLC programs, network topologies, and process variable values.
techniques.knownUse: Used across most sophisticated ICS campaigns as part of reconnaissance phase.

techniques.name: Block Command Message
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to block commands between OT devices to inhibit the ability of operators to respond to events or maintain process control.
techniques.knownUse: Industroyer/Crashoverride blocked protective relay communication during Ukraine power attack.

techniques.name: Block Reporting Message
techniques.description: Adversaries may block reporting messages to prevent operators and safety systems from receiving alerts about abnormal process conditions.
techniques.knownUse: Used to hide Stuxnet operations from SCADA operators.

techniques.name: Block Serial COM
techniques.description: Adversaries may block serial communication to OT devices to inhibit monitoring or control functions that rely on serial connectivity.
techniques.knownUse: Used in attacks targeting remote field sites connected via serial/radio.

techniques.name: Brute Force I/O
techniques.description: Adversaries may repetitively or continuously change I/O values on an ICS device to cause process disruption or physical damage through rapid cycling.
techniques.knownUse: Technique used in proof-of-concept attacks against industrial equipment.

techniques.name: Command-Line Interface
techniques.description: Adversaries may use command-line interfaces (CLI) available on OT devices and workstations to execute commands, including proprietary CLIs on HMIs and engineering tools.
techniques.knownUse: Common post-compromise technique for lateral movement and execution in OT.

techniques.name: Control Device
techniques.description: Adversaries may send commands directly to OT control devices (PLCs, RTUs) to change output values, modify setpoints, or alter process state outside of normal operator actions.
techniques.knownUse: Core technique in all process manipulation attacks. Stuxnet, Industroyer, TRITON all used this.

techniques.name: Data Destruction
techniques.description: Adversaries may destroy data and files in an OT environment to disrupt operations, prevent recovery, or cause physical consequences from loss of control data.
techniques.knownUse: Used in EKANS/SNAKE ransomware which specifically targeted OT historian and SCADA databases.

techniques.name: Data Exfiltration over C2 Channel
techniques.description: Adversaries may exfiltrate OT data including configurations, PLC programs, process data, and network topology over the command and control channel.
techniques.knownUse: Pre-operational reconnaissance in most sophisticated ICS campaigns.

techniques.name: Data from Information Repositories
techniques.description: Adversaries may collect OT configuration data from repositories such as historians, document management systems, and engineering archives.
techniques.knownUse: Attackers target historian databases for process knowledge before executing attacks.

techniques.name: Default Credentials
techniques.description: Adversaries may use default credentials (username/password combinations shipped by the vendor) to access OT devices. Default credentials are extensively documented in public vendor manuals.
techniques.knownUse: Default credentials used in Mirai botnet targeting industrial routers, Triton initial access, and numerous opportunistic attacks against internet-exposed HMIs.

techniques.name: Denial of Control
techniques.description: Adversaries may prevent operator commands from reaching OT devices, denying the ability to control the physical process during an incident.
techniques.knownUse: Used in Ukraine 2015 and 2016 power grid attacks — operators could not re-energise breakers.

techniques.name: Denial of Service
techniques.description: Adversaries may perform denial of service attacks against OT devices or networks to disrupt availability of process control functions.
techniques.knownUse: Used as part of Industroyer attack to prevent recovery during power outage.

techniques.name: Denial of View
techniques.description: Adversaries may prevent operators from seeing current process state — either by disabling displays, blocking data flows, or injecting false values.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet showed false normal centrifuge speeds while damaging equipment.

techniques.name: Device Restart/Shutdown
techniques.description: Adversaries may restart or shutdown OT devices to disrupt process control or force devices into unsafe states.
techniques.knownUse: Used in multiple ICS attacks including Industroyer to force substation outages.

techniques.name: Drive-by Compromise
techniques.description: Adversaries may compromise OT-connected systems through web-based drive-by attacks when OT workstations browse the internet.
techniques.knownUse: Havex/Dragonfly campaign used watering hole attacks to compromise energy sector engineering workstations.

techniques.name: Engineering Workstation Compromise
techniques.description: Adversaries may compromise an engineering workstation to gain access to engineering tools, PLC programming software, and direct network access to OT devices.
techniques.knownUse: Engineering workstation compromise is a common OT attack path — provides direct access to PLC programming tools.

techniques.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
techniques.description: Adversaries may exploit vulnerabilities in internet-facing OT applications (web-based SCADA HMIs, remote access portals, OT vendor portals) to gain initial access.
techniques.knownUse: Numerous SCADA HMIs exposed to internet with known vulnerabilities regularly exploited for initial access.

techniques.name: Exploitation of Remote Services
techniques.description: Adversaries may exploit vulnerabilities in remote services (RDP, VNC, SMB) within the OT environment to move laterally.
techniques.knownUse: Colonial Pipeline attackers used compromised VPN to pivot into OT management network.

techniques.name: Modify Controller Tasking
techniques.description: Adversaries may modify tasks running on OT controllers to change the execution schedule or add malicious tasks that alter process behaviour.
techniques.knownUse: TRITON modified SIS task schedules to interfere with safety function execution.

techniques.name: External Remote Services
techniques.description: Adversaries may use external remote services (VPN, remote desktop, jump servers) as initial access points to OT environments when these services are improperly secured.
techniques.knownUse: Used in Colonial Pipeline (VPN with compromised credentials), Oldsmar water treatment (TeamViewer access).

techniques.name: Graphical User Interface
techniques.description: Adversaries may use GUI applications available on OT workstations (SCADA HMI, engineering software) to interact with control systems during an intrusion.
techniques.knownUse: Attackers in Ukraine 2016 manually operated SCADA interface to open substation breakers.

techniques.name: I/O Image
techniques.description: Adversaries may read the I/O image of a PLC to understand current process state and input/output values before executing an attack.
techniques.knownUse: Reconnaissance technique used before process manipulation attacks.

techniques.name: Location Identification
techniques.description: Adversaries may physically or digitally locate OT assets to understand the physical layout of an industrial process for attack planning.
techniques.knownUse: Physical reconnaissance of facility layout combined with digital OT asset mapping.

techniques.name: Loss of Availability
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to deny access to OT systems and data to prevent operators from maintaining process control.
techniques.knownUse: Primary impact of ransomware against OT (Colonial Pipeline, Norsk Hydro).

techniques.name: Loss of Control
techniques.description: Adversaries may seek to prevent operators from controlling the physical process — forcing either automatic or manual shutdown.
techniques.knownUse: Intended outcome of Industroyer and TRITON attacks.

techniques.name: Loss of Productivity and Revenue
techniques.description: Adversaries may disrupt OT operations to cause financial losses through production downtime, product loss, or contractual penalties.
techniques.knownUse: Primary motivation for ransomware attacks against manufacturing OT (EKANS/SNAKE).

techniques.name: Loss of Safety
techniques.description: Adversaries may compromise safety systems to remove protections against physical hazards — enabling conditions that could result in mass casualties or environmental damage.
techniques.knownUse: TRITON/TRISIS targeted the Schneider Triconex SIS specifically to disable safety shutdowns.

techniques.name: Loss of View
techniques.description: Adversaries may compromise operator visibility into process state — preventing detection of abnormal conditions or attack activity.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet maintained false normal displays for over a year while destroying centrifuges.

techniques.name: Manipulation of Control
techniques.description: Adversaries may manipulate physical process control to cause damage, incorrect process execution, or unsafe states.
techniques.knownUse: Core technique in Stuxnet (centrifuge speed manipulation), Industroyer (breaker control).

techniques.name: Manipulation of View
techniques.description: Adversaries may manipulate what operators see on HMIs and SCADA displays — showing false normal values while the process is actually compromised.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet manipulated historian records and HMI displays to hide centrifuge damage.

techniques.name: Modify Alarm Settings
techniques.description: Adversaries may modify alarm thresholds or disable alarms to prevent operators from detecting abnormal process conditions caused by an attack.
techniques.knownUse: Alarm modification used in conjunction with process manipulation to delay operator response.

techniques.name: Native API
techniques.description: Adversaries may use OT protocol native APIs (Modbus function codes, DNP3 read/write, OPC calls) to interact directly with OT devices using legitimate protocol commands.
techniques.knownUse: Used in Industroyer — each module spoke native IEC-104, IEC-101, IEC-61850-GOOSE protocols.

techniques.name: Manipulate I/O Image
techniques.description: Adversaries may manipulate the I/O image of a controller — changing values that represent process inputs/outputs to cause incorrect control decisions.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet manipulated frequency converter drive I/O image to damage centrifuges.

techniques.name: Modify Parameter
techniques.description: Adversaries may modify parameters — setpoints, thresholds, timer values, PID settings — in OT controllers to alter process behaviour.
techniques.knownUse: Core Stuxnet technique — modified centrifuge rotor speed setpoints while hiding changes.

techniques.name: Module Firmware
techniques.description: Adversaries may install modified firmware on OT device hardware modules to establish persistent access or to introduce malicious functionality.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet included a PLC rootkit that persisted across PLC program downloads.

techniques.name: Modify Control Logic
techniques.description: Adversaries may modify the control logic (ladder logic, function block diagrams, structured text) running on a PLC or DCS controller to alter process behaviour.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet modified Siemens S7-300/S7-400 PLC ladder logic. TRITON modified Triconex safety logic.

techniques.name: Module Firmware
techniques.description: Adversaries may load malicious firmware onto OT device modules — providing persistent access and the ability to modify device behaviour at the lowest level.
techniques.knownUse: Advanced persistent access technique requiring significant OT-specific expertise.

techniques.name: Network Connection Enumeration
techniques.description: Adversaries may enumerate OT network connections and topology to understand the structure of the industrial environment and identify additional targets.
techniques.knownUse: Standard reconnaissance technique used across all sophisticated ICS campaigns.

techniques.name: Network Sniffing
techniques.description: Adversaries may sniff OT network traffic to capture credentials, understand protocol usage, map assets, and intercept process data — most OT protocols transmit in cleartext.
techniques.knownUse: Passive sniffing is low-risk reconnaissance on flat OT networks — most OT protocols have no encryption.

techniques.name: Network Service Scanning
techniques.description: Adversaries may scan for open OT services (Modbus port 502, DNP3 port 20000, EtherNet/IP port 44818) to identify OT devices and understand system topology.
techniques.knownUse: Shodan and Censys regularly index internet-exposed OT devices. Internal scans used in reconnaissance phases.

techniques.name: Program Download
techniques.description: Adversaries may download modified control programs to OT devices — overwriting legitimate programs with malicious versions that alter process behaviour.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet downloaded modified centrifuge control programs. TRITON downloaded modified safety logic.

techniques.name: Program Organisation Units
techniques.description: Adversaries may use the built-in programming organisation units (function blocks, data blocks) of IEC 61131-3 PLCs to inject malicious code within otherwise legitimate programs.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet injected malicious function blocks into Siemens STEP 7 projects.

techniques.name: Program Upload
techniques.description: Adversaries may upload control programs from OT devices to collect information about the configured process logic — enabling understanding and subsequent modification.
techniques.knownUse: Standard reconnaissance step before control logic modification attacks.

techniques.name: Masquerading
techniques.description: Adversaries may disguise malicious OT commands or processes as legitimate operations to avoid detection.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet masqueraded as legitimate Siemens software. TRITON masqueraded as a SIS library file.

techniques.name: Rootkit
techniques.description: Adversaries may install rootkits on OT workstations or embedded devices to maintain access and hide malicious activity from security tools.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet included a PLC rootkit — the first known rootkit specifically for PLC firmware.

techniques.name: Scripting
techniques.description: Adversaries may use scripting languages available on OT workstations to automate attack tasks — including OT protocol scripting libraries.
techniques.knownUse: Python libraries (pymodbus, python-dnp3) enable scripted attacks against OT protocols.

techniques.name: Unauthorized Command Message
techniques.description: Adversaries may send unauthorized command messages to OT devices from an attacker-controlled system or compromised OT host.
techniques.knownUse: Used in Industroyer to send IEC-104 commands directly to substation RTUs.

techniques.name: System Firmware
techniques.description: Adversaries may modify the firmware of OT devices to establish persistent access or introduce malicious functionality that survives firmware updates.
techniques.knownUse: Advanced persistence technique. Stuxnet modified S7 firmware blocks.

techniques.name: Valid Accounts
techniques.description: Adversaries may use valid credentials — obtained through credential theft, default credentials, or previous compromise — to authenticate to OT systems.
techniques.knownUse: Used in Colonial Pipeline (compromised VPN credentials), TRITON initial access, most ICS intrusions.

techniques.name: Wireless Compromise
techniques.description: Adversaries may gain access to OT environments by compromising wireless networks or devices used for OT communication.
techniques.knownUse: Industrial WiFi and WirelessHART networks attacked in multiple proof-of-concept demonstrations.

techniques.name: Point & Tag Identification
techniques.description: Adversaries may collect tag names, point addresses, and process variable identifiers from OT systems to understand what can be manipulated.
techniques.knownUse: Reconnaissance phase of all OT manipulation attacks — understanding what tags control what process.

techniques.name: Supply Chain Compromise
techniques.description: Adversaries may compromise OT components, software, or updates during the supply chain — before they reach the operational environment.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet spread via infected Siemens software distribution; HAVEX spread via trojanised OEM software.

techniques.name: User Execution
techniques.description: Adversaries may rely on OT engineers or operators to execute malicious code — through social engineering or by compromising files they are expected to open.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet spread via infected STEP 7 project files shared between engineers.

techniques.name: Transient Cyber Asset
techniques.description: Adversaries may compromise transient assets (laptops, USB drives, portable devices) that are used to interface with OT systems for maintenance.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet propagated via USB drives used by Siemens field engineers.

techniques.name: Spearphishing Attachment
techniques.description: Adversaries may use spearphishing emails with malicious attachments targeting OT engineers, vendors, or IT staff with access to OT systems.
techniques.knownUse: Havex/Dragonfly used spearphishing against energy sector OT personnel. TRITON operators used spearphishing for initial enterprise access.

techniques.name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
techniques.description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities in OT systems to gain higher privilege levels — particularly relevant for unpatched OT workstations and HMI software.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet exploited 4 zero-days including Windows privilege escalation vulnerabilities.

techniques.name: Indicator Removal on Host
techniques.description: Adversaries may delete or modify OT system logs and indicators of compromise to cover their tracks during or after an intrusion.
techniques.knownUse: Standard attacker behaviour to hamper forensic investigation. Log deletion followed by system access.

techniques.name: Project File Infection
techniques.description: Adversaries may inject malicious code into OT engineering project files — enabling spread when projects are shared between engineers or loaded to PLCs.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet infected Siemens STEP 7 project files; spreading automatically when infected projects were loaded.

techniques.name: Loss of Safety
techniques.description: Adversaries may cause loss of safety function — the most severe OT attack outcome — potentially resulting in mass casualties, environmental catastrophe, or facility destruction.
techniques.knownUse: TRITON/TRISIS specifically targeted and nearly achieved loss of safety at a Saudi petrochemical plant.

techniques.name: Service Stop
techniques.description: Adversaries may stop OT services — SCADA servers, historian services, alarm management — to disrupt operations and hinder recovery.
techniques.knownUse: EKANS/SNAKE ransomware specifically terminated OT-related Windows services before encrypting.

techniques.name: Theft of Operational Information
techniques.description: Adversaries may steal OT operational information — process configurations, safety system logic, network topologies — for espionage or to enable future attacks.
techniques.knownUse: Dragonfly/Energetic Bear targeted energy sector OT configurations for strategic intelligence.

techniques.name: Remote System Discovery
techniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to discover remote systems connected to the OT network to identify additional targets for lateral movement or attack.
techniques.knownUse: Standard OT reconnaissance technique following initial access.

techniques.name: Autorun Image
techniques.description: Adversaries may configure OT device autorun settings to automatically execute malicious code on startup, providing persistence across reboots.
techniques.knownUse: Stuxnet used Windows autorun for USB propagation. OT device startup scripts used for persistence.
